reasoning:
To date in 2025, headline year-over-year Consumer Price Index (CPI) increases have run well below the 4.1% threshold. The monthly YoY data observed is: Jan (3.0%), Feb (2.8%), Apr (2.3%), Jul (2.7%), Aug (2.9%), and Sep (3.0%). Based on the continuity of these numbers, it is certain that unobserved months (Mar, May, Jun) did not register abrupt spikes to 4.1% only to immediately fall back down into the 2% range. 

With only October, November, and December left in 2025, a jump from 3.0% to 4.1% YoY would require an extraordinary re-acceleration of monthly inflation (averaging >0.5% over the remaining months while replacing standard or weak late-2024 prints). Current macroeconomic indicators, such as standard forecasts by economists predicting Federal Reserve rate cuts in December, strongly rule out this extreme hyper-inflationary tail risk. Giving a 5% baseline tail risk accounting for unforeseen exogenous shocks or uncorrected data artifacts.
